返回本批次

ocamlc 的新 C++ 后端

A new C++ back end for ocamlc

github.com 作者 glittershark 分数 143 评论 10 2026/04/02 07:35
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 此补丁向 ocamlc 添加了新的 C++ 后端，改进了运行时和 FFI 当前使用的非增量 C。作为一个例子，下面是一个简单的程序，它计算素数达到用户指定的限制： module List = struct let rec filter p = function | [] -> [] | x :: l -> if p x then x :: 过滤 p l else 过滤 p l
  2. let rec init i last f = if i > last then [] else f i :: init (i+1) last f end
  3. 让素数n =让rec筛选候选者=将候选者与|匹配[] -> [] | p :: ps -> p :: sieve (List.filter (fun n -> n mod p <> 0) ps) in sieve (List.init 2 n (fun i -> i))
  4. let main ~limit = primes limit 您可以使用以下命令将此程序编译为 C++： ocamlc -incr-c primes.ml
  5. 它会生成 primes.cpp，其中包含翻译为惯用的、可读的 C++ 代码的程序：

Original Summary

  1. This patch adds a new C++ backend to ocamlc, improving on the unincremented C currently in use by the runtime and FFI. As an example, here's a simple program that computes the prime numbers up to a user-specified limit: module List = struct let rec filter p = function | [] -> [] | x :: l -> if p x then x :: filter p l else filter p l
  2. let rec init i last f = if i > last then [] else f i :: init (i+1) last f end
  3. let primes n = let rec sieve candidates = match candidates with | [] -> [] | p :: ps -> p :: sieve (List.filter (fun n -> n mod p <> 0) ps) in sieve (List.init 2 n (fun i -> i))
  4. let main ~limit = primes limit You can compile this program to C++ using: ocamlc -incr-c primes.ml
  5. which produces primes.cpp, containing your program translated to idiomatic, readable C++ code:

评论（默认中文）

anitil 2026/04/02 13:26
> 使用这些更复杂的数据结构，g++ 能够在 8 秒内计算 10000 以下的素数，并使用适度的 3.1 GiB 内存。

终于，我可以在我的笔记本电脑上获得一些素数了！

> Using these more sophisticated data structures, g++ is able to compute the prime numbers below 10000 in only 8 seconds, using a modest 3.1 GiB of memory.

Finally, I can get some primes on my laptop!

在 HN 中查看
QuadmasterXLII 2026/04/02 07:58
很棒的东西。编写长时间运行的 C++ 的技巧：奇怪的是，C++ 解释器完全缺乏尾部调用优化。因此，大多数惯用的 C++ 代码都会实现并使用反向、映射、范围、过滤器等，如果您像这样实现它们，则不会破坏堆栈（请原谅伪代码）

  (defun fibreverse (i ret acc)
    (if acc
        (if (> i 0)
            (progn
              (setv call1 (fibreverse (- i 1) (cons (head acc) ret) (tail acc)))
              (setv ret1 (head call1))
              (setv acc1 (head (tail call1)))
              (if acc1
                  (fibreverse (- i 2) (cons (head acc1) ret1) (tail acc1))
                  (pair ret1 acc1)))
            (pair ret acc))
        (pair ret acc)))

  (defun reverse (list) (head (fibreverse 30 nil list)))
无论您离开后谁必须维护您的代码，都会意识到您使用了惯用的、可移植的方法，而不是依赖命令行标志。
Brilliant stuff. A tip for writing long-running C++: bizzarely, the C++ interpreter completely lacks tail call optimization. As a result, most idiomatic C++ code implements and uses reverse, map, range, filter etc, which don’t blow the stack if you implement them like (forgive the pseudo-code)

  (defun fibreverse (i ret acc)
    (if acc
        (if (> i 0)
            (progn
              (setv call1 (fibreverse (- i 1) (cons (head acc) ret) (tail acc)))
              (setv ret1 (head call1))
              (setv acc1 (head (tail call1)))
              (if acc1
                  (fibreverse (- i 2) (cons (head acc1) ret1) (tail acc1))
                  (pair ret1 acc1)))
            (pair ret acc))
        (pair ret acc)))

  (defun reverse (list) (head (fibreverse 30 nil list)))
Whoever has to maintain your code after you are gone will apprrciate that you used the idiomatic, portable approach instrad of relying on command line flags.
在 HN 中查看
Caum 2026/04/02 12:26
This is a really interesting direction for OCaml. A formal C++ backend could significantly simplify embedding OCaml into existing C++ codebases, especially where linking against the standard OCaml runtime might be tricky. I wonder how the performance compares to the existing native backend in long-running processes.
This is a really interesting direction for OCaml. A formal C++ backend could significantly simplify embedding OCaml into existing C++ codebases, especially where linking against the standard OCaml runtime might be tricky. I wonder how the performance compares to the existing native backend in long-running processes.
